Patch Set 1:

I don't think it makes sense to try and add more than 256 extents into a single RPC.  That is already a lot of random IO, and it makes the RPC size very large over the network as well as for memory allocations on the server to handle such large and fragmented I/Os.  IMHO, it would be better for the client to submit multiple RPCs with random blocks in parallel, so that they get over the network more quickly and have a chance to be merged.

Patch Set 1:

Any reply to my comment about limiting the RPC to 256 fragments, regardless of the maximum RPC size?  This limits the amount of memory needed for sending and receiving very fragmented/random writes, and also allows for more parallelism sending handling the RPC at the client and server, rather than sending up to 4096 random pages in a single RPC.

Patch Set 1:

Andreas, I was probably on the plane when you wrote your comment.

I think it makes a lot of sense to limit the number of extents in a single RPC, but the previous was unfortunately incorrect. We should count osc_extents instead of chunks in the RPC.

In order to accomplish that, we need to remember how many osc_extents have already been added to the RPC, and then set the upper limit to be 256.

parents.subject: LU-930 doc: move DLC doc to Documentation dir

commit.subject: LU-8680 osc: soft lock - osc_makes_rpc()
commit.message:
LU-8680 osc: soft lock - osc_makes_rpc()

It is possible that an osc_extent contains more than 256 chunks, and
the IO engine won't add this extent in one RPC
(try_to_add_extent_for_io) so that osc_check_rpcs() run into a loop
upon this extent and never break.

This patch changes osc_max_write_chunks() to make sure the value
can cover all possible osc_extent, so that all osc_extent will be
added into one RPC.

This patch also add another field erd_max_extents in extent_rpc_data
to make sure not to add too many fragments in a single RPC.

Signed-off-by: Bobi Jam <bobijam.xu@intel.com>
Change-Id: Icf58a6bd04655bb9aa5589dd002e118c21ed932d
Reviewed-on: http://review.whamcloud.com/23326
Reviewed-by: Jinshan Xiong <jinshan.xiong@intel.com>
Tested-by: Jenkins
Tested-by: Maloo <hpdd-maloo@intel.com>
Reviewed-by: Niu Yawei <yawei.niu@intel.com>
Reviewed-by: Oleg Drokin <oleg.drokin@intel.com>
